moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with the Zukertort Opening, one of the most flexible first moves in chess. Instead of claiming the center immediately with 1.e4 or 1.d4, we develop our knight to an excellent square that controls e5 and d4, keeping our options open. This move was popularized by Wilhelm Zukertort in the 19th century and allows us to build our position methodically while maintaining strategic flexibility—we can follow up with d4, e4, or even c4 depending on Black's response.

Black prepares to support the e5 advance and keep our d4 square under pressure. This modest pawn move is characteristic of the Caro-Kann-style setup and signals that Black isn't interested in immediate central confrontation. The move also prepares to develop the bishop actively later, though it does consume tempo compared to more forcing defenses.

Now we stake our claim in the center with the natural follow-up, establishing a strong pawn on d4 that controls critical central squares. This move forces Black to make an immediate decision about the center, and we're giving notice that we want real tension in the middle of the board. By playing Nf3 first, we've ensured our knight is already well-placed to support this pawn push.

Black strikes back in the center with this gambit move, challenging our d4 pawn and opening lines across the board. This is the Vos Gambit—Black sacrifices the e5 pawn to accelerate their counterplay and create immediate tactical opportunities. Black hopes that after we capture on e5, they'll have active piece play and central control that justifies the pawn, a typical gambit strategy where initiative compensates for material.
